en.extract:
The orange-crowned warbler (Leiothlypis celata) is a small songbird of the New World warbler family.


Taxonomy
The orange-crowned warbler was formally described in 1822 by the American zoologist Thomas Say under the binomial name Sylvia celatus from a specimen collected on an expedition from Pittsburgh to the Rocky Mountains led by Stephen Harriman Long. The specific epithet celatus is Latin and means "secret" or "hidden". The type locality is Omaha, Nebraska. The orange-crowned warbler is now placed in the genus Leiothlypis that was introduced by the Dutch ornithologist George Sangster in 2008. The genus name is derived from the Ancient Greek λειος/leios meaning "plain" and θλυπις/thlupis, an unknown small bird mentioned by Aristotle.
Four subspecies are recognised:

L. c. celata (Say, 1822) – breeds in central Alaska to south Canada, winters in Guatemala
L. c. lutescens (Ridgway, 1872) – breeds in west Canada and west USA, winters in Guatemala
L. c. orestera (Oberholser, 1905) – breeds in west central Canada and west central USA, winters in Mexico
L. c. sordida (Townsend, CH, 1890) – resident in south California (southwest USA) and northwest Mexico


Description
The orange-crowned warbler has olive-grey upperparts, yellowish underparts with faint streaking and a thin pointed bill. It has a faint line over each eye and a faint broken eye ring.  The orange patch on the crown is usually not visible. Females and immatures are duller in colour than males. Western birds are yellower than eastern birds. Orange-crowned warblers are distinguished by their lack of wing bars, streaking on the underparts, strong face marking or bright colouring, resembling a fall Tennessee warbler and a black-throated blue warbler, both of which are also members of the New World warbler family.
The song of is a trill, descending in pitch and volume. The call is a high chip.


Distribution and habitat
Their breeding habitat is open shrubby areas across Canada, Alaska and the western United States. These birds migrate in the winter to the southern United States and south to Central America. Although they are quite common in the western United States, they are uncommon in the east.


Behaviour and ecology


Breeding

The nest is a small open cup well-concealed on the ground under vegetation or low in shrubs. The female builds the nest; four to six eggs are laid in a nest on the ground or in a low bush. Both parents feed the young.


Food and feeding
They forage actively in low shrubs, flying from perch to perch, sometimes hovering. These birds eat insects, berries and nectar.


References


External links
Orange-crowned warbler - Island Misfits at Smithsonian Migratory Bird Center
Orange-crowned warbler species account - Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Orange-crowned warbler - Vermivora celata Archived 2021-03-18 at the Wayback Machine - USGS Patuxent Bird Identification InfoCenter
BirdLife species factsheet for Vermivora celata
"Oreothlypis celata". Avibase. 
"Orange-crowned warbler media". Internet Bird Collection.
Orange-crowned warbler photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)
Audio recordings of Orange-crowned warbler on Xeno-canto.

The genus name is derived from the [[Ancient Greek]] λειος/''leios'' meaning "plain" and θλυπις/''thlupis'', an unknown small bird mentioned by [[Aristotle]].{{ cite journal | last=Sangster | first=George | author-link=George Sangster | year=2008 | title=A revision of ''Vermivora'' (Parulidae) with the description of a new genus | journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club | volume=128 | pages=207–211 [210–211] | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/45653808 }}{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | year=2020 | title=New World warblers, mitrospingid tanagers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 10.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/warblers/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=2 October 2020 }} Four [[subspecies]] are recognised: * ''L. c. celata'' ([[Thomas Say|Say]], 1822) – breeds in central Alaska to south Canada, winters in Guatemala * ''L. c. lutescens'' ([[Robert Ridgway|Ridgway]], 1872) – breeds in west Canada and west USA, winters in Guatemala * ''L. c. orestera'' ([[Harry C. Oberholser|Oberholser]], 1905) – breeds in west central Canada and west central USA, winters in Mexico * ''L. c. sordida'' ([[Charles Haskins Townsend|Townsend, CH]], 1890) – resident in south California (southwest USA) and northwest Mexico == Description == The orange-crowned warbler has olive-grey upperparts, yellowish underparts with faint streaking and a thin pointed bill. It has a faint line over each eye and a faint broken eye ring. The orange patch on the crown is usually not visible. Females and immatures are duller in colour than males. Western birds are yellower than eastern birds. Orange-crowned warblers are distinguished by their lack of wing bars, streaking on the underparts, strong face marking or bright colouring, resembling a fall [[Tennessee warbler]] and a [[black-throated blue warbler]], both of which are also members of the New World warbler family. The song of is a trill, descending in pitch and volume. These birds eat insects, berries and nectar. ==References== {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book |last=Godfrey |first=W. Earl |date=1966 |title=The Birds of Canada |location=Ottawa |publisher=National Museum of Canada |page=323}} {{cite book |last=Sibley |first=David Allen |author-link=David Allen Sibley |date=2000 |title=The Sibley Guide to Birds |location=New York |publisher=Knopf |page=[https://archive.org/details/sibleyguidetobir00sibl_0/page/427 427] |isbn=0-679-45122-6 |url-access=registration |url=https://archive.org/details/sibleyguidetobir00sibl_0/page/427 }} }} {{refbegin}} * {{cite journal |last1=Peluc |first1=S.I. |last2=Sillett |first2=T.S. |last3=Rotenberry |first3=J.T. |last4=Ghalambor |first4=C.K. |year=2008 |title=Adaptive plasticity in nest site selection in response to increased predation risk |journal=Behavioral Ecology |volume=19 |pages=830–835 |doi=10.1093/beheco/arn033|doi-access=free }} {{refend}} ==External links== * [https://web.archive.org/web/20140619231509/http://nationalzoo.si.edu/scbi/migratorybirds/featured_birds/?id=306 Orange-crowned warbler - Island Misfits] at [http://nationalzoo.si.edu/scbi/migratorybirds/ Smithsonian Migratory Bird Center] * [https://www.birds.cornell.edu/AllAboutBirds/BirdGuide/Orange-crowned_Warbler.html Orange-crowned warbler species account] - Cornell Lab of Ornithology * [https://www.mbr-pwrc.usgs.gov/id/framlst/i6460id.html Orange-crowned warbler - ''Vermivora celata''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210318152337/http://www.mbr-pwrc.usgs.gov/id/framlst/i6460id.html |date=2021-03-18 }} - USGS Patuxent Bird Identification InfoCenter * {{BirdLife|22721624|Vermivora celata}} * {{Avibase|name=Oreothlypis celata}} * {{InternetBirdCollection|orange-crowned-warbler-vermivora-celata|Orange-crowned warbler}} * {{VIREO|Orange-crowned+warbler}} * {{Xeno-canto species|Leiothlypis|celata|Orange-crowned warbler}} {{Taxonbar|from=Q27075888}} [[Category:Leiothlypis]] [[Category:Birds of North America]] [[Category:Birds described in 1823|orange-crowned warbler]] [[Category:Taxa named by Thomas Say]]

zh.extract:
橙冠蟲森鶯（學名：Leiothlypis celata；英語：Orange-crowned warbler）是雷奧蟲森鶯屬的一種小型鳥類，分佈於北美洲。


特征

雖然叫做橙冠蟲森鶯，但其頭部的橙色一般是看不到的。雄鳥羽毛更加鮮亮，而其中分佈於西部的橙冠蟲森鶯羽毛又比東部的橙冠蟲森鶯更加鮮亮。


參考文獻


外部連接
